03

The Data Highway

From URL paste to finished cover letter — trace every handoff in the pipeline.

When you paste a URL, what happens in the next 20 seconds?

Think of the system as a postal sorting facility. Mail arrives (your job posting URL), gets read and stamped (intent detection), routed to the right department (fetcher, cleaner, AI engine), and eventually delivered as a polished package (cover letter + JSON summary). Nothing happens by magic — every step has a specific job and hands off to the next worker with a clean baton.

The full journey touches six distinct components, crosses three file-system boundaries, and makes exactly one call to the Claude API. Everything else is plain Python doing what Python does best: string manipulation, HTTP requests, and file I/O.

📮
The sorting-facility metaphor

Incoming mail = raw user message. Sorter = intent parser. Conveyor = orchestrator. Specialist worker = Claude. Outgoing post = exported files. Each worker only sees what they need — no one reads the whole letter.

The Six-Stage Pipeline

Click Next Step to walk through each handoff. Watch which component lights up and what data travels across the wire.

⌨️
User Input
🔍
Intent Parser
🌐
JD Fetcher
🎛️
Orchestrator
🤖
Claude API
💾
Export
Click "Next Step" to begin

How Does the System Know What You Want?

The intent parser runs a strict priority chain: URL check first, then URL pattern match, then keyword checks, then length. Order matters — a message with both a URL and the word "help" is treated as a URL job, not a help request.

CODE 

def parse_intent(message):
    message = message.strip()
    url_match = _URL_PATTERN.search(message)
    if url_match:
        return ParsedIntent(
            intent=Intent.ANALYZE_URL,
            url=url_match.group(0)
        )
    if _HISTORY_PATTERN.search(message):
        return ParsedIntent(intent=Intent.SHOW_HISTORY)
    if _HELP_PATTERN.search(message) and len(message) < 50:
        return ParsedIntent(intent=Intent.HELP)
    if len(message) >= 100:
        return ParsedIntent(
            intent=Intent.ANALYZE_TEXT,
            jd_text=message
        )
    return ParsedIntent(intent=Intent.UNKNOWN)
PLAIN ENGLISH

Strip whitespace so accidental spaces don't fool the checks.

Run the URL regex. If any URL shape is found, immediately return an ANALYZE_URL intent — URL always wins, no matter what else the message says.

Look for words like "history", "past", "previous". If found, return SHOW_HISTORY.

Look for "help" or "?" — but only if the message is short (under 50 chars). A long message with the word "help" is probably a JD, not a help request.

If the message is 100+ characters and nothing else matched, treat it as a raw job description pasted directly.

If none of the above matched, return UNKNOWN — the bot will ask for clarification.

💡
Why the 100-character threshold?

Real job descriptions are almost never shorter than a tweet. The 100-char floor keeps the system from treating a short confused message like "analyse senior data role at Google" as a full JD — it falls through to UNKNOWN and prompts the user to provide more.

How the Fetcher Cleans Raw HTML

A raw job-posting page is mostly clutter — navigation menus, cookie banners, footer links. The fetcher uses BeautifulSoup to surgically remove the noise and surface the actual job content — then caps it at 8 000 characters to keep token costs reasonable.

CODE 

def _extract_text(html):
    soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")
    for tag in soup(["script", "style",
                    "nav", "footer", "header"]):
        tag.decompose()
    main_content = (
        soup.find("main")
        or soup.find("article")
        or soup.find(id=re.compile(
            r"job|description|content", re.I))
    )
    target = main_content if main_content else soup.body or soup
    lines = [l.strip() for l in
             target.get_text(separator="\n").splitlines()]
    return "\n".join(l for l in lines if l)[:8000]
PLAIN ENGLISH

Parse the raw HTML string into a navigable tree.

Find every <script>, <style>, <nav>, <footer>, and <header> tag and delete them from the tree entirely. This removes JavaScript, CSS, menus, and footer links in one pass.

Try to find the most content-dense element: a <main> tag first, then <article>, then any element whose id contains "job", "description", or "content" (case-insensitive).

If no landmark element was found, fall back to <body> — or the whole document if there is no body.

Extract all visible text, split on newlines, strip each line of whitespace, and drop empty lines. This collapses all the messy HTML into clean paragraphs.

Hard-cap the result at 8 000 characters. Anything beyond that is usually boilerplate or repeated footer content anyway.

Check Your Understanding

Two quick scenarios. Read carefully — the details matter.

Scenario

A user sends the 10-character message: "help me". No URL, no history keyword. What does the intent parser return?

Which intent does the parser assign?

Scenario

The fetcher calls tag.decompose() on every <nav> and <footer> element before extracting text.

Why does the fetcher remove <nav> and <footer> tags?
